source_item.title: From a Class 10 Dropout to Making Parts for Rolls-Royce, Boeing and Fighter Jets

hook.label: India's **Dropout** Is Making Parts for Rolls-Royce
hook.subhead: From a Hyderabad shed to a $1.8B company.
hook.image_scene: A lone CNC machine running inside a small, dimly lit corrugated-iron shed in an industrial area, metal shavings scattered on the concrete floor, single fluorescent tube overhead

beats.label: THE BACKSTORY
beats.headline: **Failed** Class 10. Called a Failure.
beats.body: Rakesh Chopdar couldn't pass his 10th-grade exams. Family and friends labeled him "incapable." So he went to work in his father's nut-and-bolt factory in Hyderabad instead.
beats.image_scene: A worn school report card lying on a workbench next to a spanner and a grease rag, shot close up under harsh workshop light

beats.label: THE MOMENT EVERYTHING CHANGED
beats.headline: One Customer. One Part. **One Chance.**
beats.body: A man walked into the factory carrying an aerofoil — a blade used inside jet engines and turbines. He'd traveled the world looking for a good supplier. Rakesh asked for a few months to try making one himself. He delivered. The customer was amazed.
beats.image_scene: A gleaming titanium aerofoil blade held up by two hands against a blurred workshop background, light catching its curved edge

beats.label: FROM SHED TO GLOBAL SUPPLIER
beats.headline: Started With **One Machine** in 200 Sq Ft
beats.body: In 2008, Rakesh started AZAD Engineering in a 200-square-meter shed in Balanagar, Hyderabad, with one second-hand CNC machine and revenue of just ₹2 crore.
beats.image_scene: An aerial view of a modern multi-building manufacturing campus in Hyderabad at dusk, lights on in every factory block, expansion plots visible next to it

timeline.text: Started in a 200 sq m shed. Revenue: ₹2 crore.
IPO lists at 37% premium. Market cap crosses $1B.

beats.label: WHO TRUSTS AZAD TODAY
beats.headline: The World's **Biggest Names** in Aerospace
beats.image_scene: A close-up of a precision-machined turbine component with a Rolls-Royce inspection tag attached, sitting on a quality-control inspection table under bright white light

beats.label: THE REAL STORY HERE
beats.headline: The West **Didn't Believe** India Could Do This
beats.body: These aren't cheap commodity parts. They must meet a zero-defects standard. For decades, global aerospace primes sourced this kind of work only from US, European, and Japanese factories. A self-taught guy from Hyderabad just proved them wrong. And the order book now sits above ₹6,500 crore.
beats.image_scene: A quality inspector in a white coat and gloves measuring a precision aerospace component with a coordinate measuring machine, focus on the instrument's digital readout showing microscopic tolerances

engage.label: YOUR TAKE
engage.headline: If India can hit **zero defects** for Rolls-Royce, why do we still assume the West owns deep manufacturing?
engage.cta: Drop your thoughts below. Follow for more stories like this.
engage.image_scene: Rakesh Chopdar-style moment: a man in a factory uniform standing with arms crossed in front of a row of humming CNC machines, looking directly at the camera, confident expression, Hyderabad factory floor setting
